_meta.note: POC corpus (neurology). DRAFT patient-education content grounded in PUBLIC-DOMAIN sources (NINDS, MedlinePlus, CDC) plus general clinical patient-education knowledge. NOT clinically reviewed — every canonical_answer MUST be reviewed and owned by a clinician (set reviewed_by + last_reviewed) before ANY non-internal/patient exposure. Answers are deliberately general and defer on anything individualized (specific doses, personal timelines). Emergency thresholds (seizure 911 criteria, BE-FAST) grounded against CDC/MedlinePlus 2026-06-29. See faq_sources_bibliography.md.
_meta.escalation_legend: none = answer plainly | soft = answer + defer-to-prescriber/team | route = handled by guardrails (not stored here)

entries.question: What should I expect in the first few weeks of stroke recovery?
entries.canonical_answer: Recovery is different for everyone. The early weeks usually focus on rehabilitation, which may include physical, occupational, and speech therapy, and on gradually rebuilding strength and daily function. Fatigue and emotional ups and downs are common in this period.

entries.question: When can I drive after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving after a stroke depends on how it affected your vision, movement, thinking, and reaction time, and many places require a waiting period and a clinician's clearance. Most people are asked not to drive until their care team has evaluated them.

entries.question: How can I lower my risk of another stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, lowering stroke risk focuses on managing blood pressure, taking prescribed medications consistently, not smoking, staying active, and eating a heart-healthy diet. Your care team will tailor a prevention plan to you, so it's worth asking which steps matter most in your case.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel very tired after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Fatigue is very common after a stroke and can last weeks to months as your brain and body recover, and pacing activities with rest often helps. But if you suddenly develop new symptoms such as face drooping, weakness or numbness on one side, trouble speaking, or vision changes, call 911 right away, since those can be signs of another stroke.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel sad or emotional after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Mood changes, including sadness, anxiety, frustration, and tearfulness, are common after a stroke and can have both emotional and physical causes. This is treatable, so please tell your care team how you're feeling, especially if it's persistent.

entries.question: When is my follow-up appointment after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Follow-up timing varies, but many people are seen within a few weeks of leaving the hospital, sometimes with neurology, primary care, and therapy visits. Your discharge paperwork should list your specific appointments.

entries.question: What stroke warning signs should make me call 911?
entries.canonical_answer: Use BE-FAST: sudden Balance loss, Eye or vision changes, Face drooping, Arm weakness, Speech trouble. The T is Time — call 911 right away if any of these appear suddenly, even if they go away. Other warning signs include sudden numbness, confusion, or a sudden severe headache.

entries.question: What is a TIA or mini-stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: A TIA, sometimes called a mini-stroke, is a brief episode of stroke-like symptoms that goes away on its own. Even though it resolves, it is a medical emergency that needs immediate evaluation in an emergency room, because it can signal a high risk of a full stroke in the days that follow. If you are having stroke-like symptoms now, call 911.

entries.question: Can I exercise after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Most people are encouraged to gradually become more active after a stroke, often starting with a therapy or rehab program. The right type and pace of exercise depends on your recovery, so check with your care team before starting.

entries.question: When can I go back to work after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Returning to work depends on your recovery, your job's demands, and how the stroke affected you. Some people return within weeks, others need longer or a gradual return. Your care team and any therapists can help guide the timing.

entries.question: What are my stroke medications generally for?
entries.canonical_answer: After a stroke, people are often prescribed medicines that fall into a few general groups: blood thinners or antiplatelets to reduce clot risk, blood-pressure medicines, and cholesterol-lowering medicines like statins. Taking them as prescribed is an important part of preventing another stroke; ask your prescriber what each of yours does.

entries.question: Is there a recommended diet after a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, a heart-healthy eating pattern is recommended after a stroke: plenty of vegetables and fruit, whole grains, and less salt, saturated fat, and processed food. If you have swallowing problems or other conditions, your care team or a dietitian can tailor advice for you.

entries.question: What do my follow-up blood tests check?
entries.canonical_answer: Follow-up bloodwork in neurology often checks things like medication levels, kidney and liver function, blood counts, or cholesterol and blood sugar, depending on your condition and medicines. Your care team orders them based on what they're monitoring for you.

entries.question: Why do I need a follow-up MRI or CT scan?
entries.canonical_answer: Repeat imaging is often used to see how something is healing or changing over time, to compare against an earlier scan, or to check that treatment is working. Your care team can tell you the specific reason yours was ordered.

entries.question: What should I bring to my neurology appointment?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps to bring a current list of your medications and doses, any symptom notes or a diary, your insurance and ID, results or records from other doctors if you have them, and a list of questions. Bringing a family member or caregiver can also help.

entries.question: How do I get my test results?
entries.canonical_answer: Test results are usually shared through your patient portal, by a call or message from the office, or at your next visit. If you haven't heard back and are waiting on results, it's reasonable to contact the clinic to ask.

entries.question: What happens after my neurology clinic visit?
entries.canonical_answer: After a visit you'll usually get a summary of the plan, which may include new or changed medications, tests to schedule, referrals, and when to follow up. If anything in your after-visit summary is unclear, the clinic can clarify it.

entries.question: How do I refill my prescription?
entries.canonical_answer: Refills are usually requested through your pharmacy or your patient portal, and the office renews them if appropriate. Request refills before you run out, since some medicines should not be stopped suddenly. Your clinic can tell you their preferred way to handle refills.

entries.question: Who do I call if I have questions between visits?
entries.canonical_answer: For non-urgent questions, your neurology clinic or its nurse line is usually the right contact, often through the patient portal or phone. For anything urgent or an emergency, call your provider's urgent line or 911. When in doubt, it's always okay to call and ask.

entries.question: How do I prepare for my first neurology appointment?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps to write down your main symptoms and when they started, bring your medication list and any prior test results, and note questions you want to ask. Arriving a little early to handle paperwork can make the visit smoother.

entries.question: What's the difference between a neurologist and a neurosurgeon?
entries.canonical_answer: A neurologist is a doctor who diagnoses and treats conditions of the brain, spine, and nerves, usually without surgery, often using medicines and other therapies. A neurosurgeon is a doctor who performs surgery on the brain, spine, and nerves. Many patients see both as part of their care.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel tired or confused after a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Yes. After a seizure many people have a recovery period, sometimes called the postictal phase, with tiredness, confusion, soreness, or not remembering the event. This can last minutes and sometimes longer. If recovery seems unusually long or different from your past seizures, tell your care team.

entries.question: When is a seizure an emergency?
entries.canonical_answer: Call 911 if a seizure lasts more than about 5 minutes, if another seizure starts soon after one ends, if the person does not wake up or return to normal afterward, if it's their first-ever seizure, if it happens in water, or if they're injured, pregnant, or have trouble breathing. When in doubt, call 911.

entries.question: What should I do if someone is having a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Help them to the ground in a safe spot, move away hard or sharp objects, cushion the head, loosen anything tight around the neck, and gently turn them on their side. Do not hold them down or put anything in their mouth, and stay with them until they recover. Call 911 if the seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, if another starts before they recover, if they do not wake up, if it is their first seizure, or if they are injured, pregnant, in water, or having trouble breathing.

entries.question: What should I do if I miss a dose of my seizure medicine?
entries.canonical_answer: Missing doses of seizure medicine can raise the risk of a seizure, so it's important to take it consistently. What to do for a specific missed dose depends on the medicine and timing, so check the instructions that came with it or call your pharmacist or prescriber. Don't double up unless they tell you to.

entries.question: Can I drink alcohol with my seizure medication?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, alcohol can interact with seizure medicines and can itself trigger seizures, so many people are advised to limit or avoid it. Because this depends on your specific medicine and situation, confirm with the doctor who prescribed it.

entries.question: When can I drive if I have seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving rules after a seizure vary by location and usually require a seizure-free period and sometimes a doctor's sign-off. Because the specifics depend on where you live and your situation, check with your provider and your local driving authority before driving.

entries.question: What can trigger a seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: Common triggers for people with epilepsy include missed medication, lack of sleep, high stress, alcohol, and sometimes flashing lights or illness. Triggers vary from person to person, so it can help to track yours and share them with your care team.

entries.question: Will I have to take seizure medication forever?
entries.canonical_answer: It depends on the cause of your seizures and how well they're controlled. Some people take medication long term, while others may eventually reduce or stop under a doctor's guidance. This is a decision to make with your neurologist, and you should not stop on your own.

entries.question: What should my family know about my seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps for family to know basic seizure first aid: keep you safe, cushion your head, turn you on your side, time the seizure, and never put anything in your mouth. They should also know to call 911 if a seizure lasts more than 5 minutes, if another begins before you recover, if you do not wake up, if you are injured or having trouble breathing, or if it happens in water.

entries.question: What does levetiracetam (Keppra) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Levetiracetam, sold as Keppra, is a medicine used to help prevent seizures. It acts on electrical activity in the brain to make seizures less likely, and it's taken on a regular schedule to keep a steady level in your body.

entries.question: What does lamotrigine (Lamictal) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Lamotrigine, sold as Lamictal, is used to help prevent seizures and, in some people, to help stabilize mood. It's usually started at a low dose and increased slowly. A new rash while taking lamotrigine can rarely be a sign of a serious reaction, so if you develop a rash, especially with fever, blistering, or sores in the mouth or eyes, seek emergency care right away.

entries.question: What does topiramate (Topamax) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Topiramate, sold as Topamax, is used to help prevent seizures and to help prevent migraines in some people. It's taken on a schedule, and your prescriber can explain what to watch for while taking it.

entries.question: What does gabapentin do?
entries.canonical_answer: Gabapentin is used for certain types of nerve pain and for some seizure conditions. It works by calming overactive nerve signaling. It's taken on a schedule, and your prescriber can tell you what to expect.

entries.question: Why am I taking aspirin after my stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Low-dose aspirin is often used after certain strokes as an antiplatelet, meaning it helps keep blood platelets from clumping into clots, which can lower the risk of another stroke. Because aspirin isn't right for everyone, take it exactly as your prescriber directed.

entries.question: What does clopidogrel (Plavix) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Clopidogrel, sold as Plavix, is an antiplatelet medicine that helps prevent blood clots, and it's often used to lower the risk of another stroke or related events. Take it as prescribed and tell your providers you're on it, since it affects bleeding.

entries.question: Why am I on a statin like atorvastatin after my stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: Statins such as atorvastatin lower cholesterol and help stabilize blood vessels, which can reduce the risk of another stroke or heart event. They're taken long term in most cases; your prescriber can explain your specific reason and any monitoring.

entries.question: How do I know if my medication is causing side effects?
entries.canonical_answer: Many medicines have common, usually mild side effects listed in the information that comes with them. If you notice new or bothersome symptoms after starting a medicine, it's worth checking that leaflet and contacting your prescriber or pharmacist. Severe reactions, like trouble breathing or a spreading rash, need emergency care.

entries.question: Can I stop my medication once I feel better?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, you should not stop a neurology medication on your own, even if you feel better, because some medicines need to be continued or tapered slowly and stopping suddenly can cause problems. Talk with the doctor who prescribed it before making any change.

entries.question: Can I take over-the-counter medicines with my neurology medications?
entries.canonical_answer: Some over-the-counter medicines, supplements, and herbal products can interact with neurology medications. Before starting a new one, it's a good idea to check with your pharmacist or prescriber, who can look for interactions.

entries.question: When can I drive after a craniotomy?
entries.canonical_answer: Driving after brain surgery depends on your recovery, whether you've had seizures, your vision and reaction time, and sometimes local rules, so there isn't a single timeline. Your neurosurgeon will tell you when it's safe for you, and it's best not to drive until they clear you.

entries.question: When do my stitches or staples come out after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Stitches or staples after a craniotomy are usually removed at a follow-up visit once the incision has healed enough, and the exact timing is set by your surgeon. Your discharge instructions should give your specific date, so check there or call the surgeon's office.

entries.question: Are there lifting restrictions after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: People are usually asked to avoid heavy lifting and straining for a period after a craniotomy, because it can raise pressure in the head. The specific weight limit and how long it lasts come from your surgeon, so follow your discharge instructions.

entries.question: When can I shower or wash my hair after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Many people can shower a few days after surgery but are asked to keep the incision care simple and gentle for a couple of weeks, sometimes avoiding certain hair products at first. Because instructions vary by surgeon and incision, follow the discharge guidance you were given.

entries.question: How do I care for my incision after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: General incision care usually means keeping it clean and dry, following your surgeon's instructions on dressings, and watching for signs of infection like increasing redness, swelling, warmth, drainage, or fever. If you see those signs, contact your surgeon's office; for severe symptoms, seek urgent care.

entries.question: Is it normal to feel very tired after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Fatigue is common and can last weeks after a craniotomy as your brain heals, and resting and gradually increasing activity usually helps. But a suddenly worsening headache, new confusion, fever, repeated vomiting, weakness, or vision changes after brain surgery can be an emergency, so call your surgeon's on-call line or seek emergency care right away.

entries.question: When can I return to work after a craniotomy?
entries.canonical_answer: Return-to-work timing after brain surgery varies a lot depending on the surgery, your recovery, and your job. Some people return in a few weeks and others need longer. Your neurosurgeon can give you guidance based on your situation.

entries.question: Is some headache normal after brain surgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Mild headache can be expected for a while after a craniotomy as you heal. However, a severe, rapidly worsening, or new type of headache, especially with fever, vomiting, confusion, vision changes, or weakness, is not something to wait on, contact your surgeon or seek urgent care.

entries.question: When can I fly after neurosurgery?
entries.canonical_answer: Air travel after brain surgery is usually delayed for a period and depends on your specific surgery and recovery. Because there isn't a single rule, ask your neurosurgeon when flying is safe for you before booking travel.

entries.question: Why did they order both a CT and an MRI?
entries.canonical_answer: CT and MRI show different things. A CT scan is fast and good at detecting bleeding, while an MRI gives more detailed pictures of brain tissue and can show changes a CT may miss. Ordering both is common and gives the team a fuller picture.

entries.question: Is a headache normal a few days after a lumbar puncture?
entries.canonical_answer: A headache after a lumbar puncture is a known and usually self-limited effect, often worse when sitting or standing and better when lying down. Resting and staying hydrated can help. But a severe headache with fever, a stiff neck, confusion, or vision changes can be a sign of a serious problem such as meningitis, so for those, seek emergency care right away rather than waiting.

entries.question: What is a lumbar puncture or spinal tap?
entries.canonical_answer: A lumbar puncture, or spinal tap, is a test where a thin needle is used to collect a small sample of the fluid around the spinal cord, usually from the lower back. It helps diagnose certain infections, bleeding, and other neurological conditions. Your team can explain why yours was done.

entries.question: What is an EEG?
entries.canonical_answer: An EEG, or electroencephalogram, is a painless test that records the brain's electrical activity using small sensors placed on the scalp. It's often used to evaluate seizures and some other conditions. Your team can tell you how to prepare for yours.

entries.question: What is an EMG or nerve conduction study?
entries.canonical_answer: An EMG and nerve conduction study are tests that check how well your nerves and muscles are working, often used to evaluate numbness, weakness, or nerve pain. They involve small electrical pulses and sometimes a thin needle, and any discomfort is usually brief.

entries.question: What is an MRI with contrast?
entries.canonical_answer: An MRI with contrast uses a special dye, usually given through a vein, to make certain tissues or problems show up more clearly on the scan. It's commonly used and your team chooses it when the extra detail is helpful. Tell the team about kidney problems or past reactions to contrast.

entries.question: How do I prepare for an MRI?
entries.canonical_answer: For most brain MRIs you can eat normally beforehand unless told otherwise, but you'll need to remove metal objects and tell the staff about any implants, devices, or metal in your body. If you get claustrophobic, let your team know in advance, since there are ways to help.

entries.question: What is a migraine?
entries.canonical_answer: A migraine is a type of headache that is often moderate to severe and can come with nausea or sensitivity to light and sound, and sometimes warning symptoms called an aura. It's a common neurological condition with many treatment options, which your care team can discuss with you.

entries.question: When should I worry about a headache?
entries.canonical_answer: Most headaches aren't dangerous, but some warning signs deserve urgent attention: a sudden severe headache or the worst headache of your life, a headache with weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, vision loss, confusion, fever with a stiff neck, or one after a head injury. For those, call 911 or seek emergency care.

entries.question: What is neuropathy?
entries.canonical_answer: Neuropathy means damage or dysfunction of the nerves, often the ones in the hands and feet, which can cause numbness, tingling, burning, or weakness. It has many causes, and your care team can help find the cause and discuss ways to manage it.

entries.question: When can I drive again?
entries.canonical_answer: Whether and when you can drive depends on your specific condition, such as recent seizures, surgery, stroke, vision changes, or certain medications, and sometimes on local rules. Because it's individual, your care team is the right source for when driving is safe for you.

entries.question: What is dementia?
entries.canonical_answer: Dementia is a general term for a decline in memory and thinking that is serious enough to affect daily life, and it has several causes. Some contributing factors are treatable, so an evaluation is worthwhile if you or your family are concerned.

entries.question: Is my forgetfulness normal aging or dementia?
entries.canonical_answer: Occasionally forgetting a name or where you put your keys is common with aging, while dementia tends to involve worsening memory and thinking that interferes with everyday activities. Because the line can be hard to judge, an evaluation by your care team is the best way to know.

entries.question: How do I prepare for a memory evaluation?
entries.canonical_answer: It helps to bring a list of your medications, specific examples of the changes you've noticed and when they started, any prior test results, and ideally a family member who knows your day-to-day. Writing down your questions ahead of time is useful too.

entries.question: What is Parkinson's disease?
entries.canonical_answer: Parkinson's disease is a movement disorder that can cause a tremor at rest, stiffness, slowness, and balance changes, and it develops gradually. It's a treatable condition with several options, which your neurologist can discuss with you.

entries.question: What's the difference between essential tremor and Parkinson's?
entries.canonical_answer: In general, essential tremor tends to happen when you're using your hands, like holding a cup or writing, while a Parkinson's tremor is often most noticeable at rest and comes with other features like stiffness or slowness. They're different conditions, and an exam is the best way to tell them apart.

entries.question: Why does the timing of my carbidopa-levodopa matter?
entries.canonical_answer: Carbidopa-levodopa works best when taken on a consistent schedule, and for some people food, especially protein, can affect how well a dose works. Because the right timing is individual, follow the plan your prescriber gave you and ask them before changing it.

entries.question: What is multiple sclerosis?
entries.canonical_answer: Multiple sclerosis, or MS, is a condition where the immune system affects the protective covering of nerves in the brain and spinal cord, which can cause symptoms like numbness, weakness, vision changes, or balance problems. It's managed with a range of treatments your neurologist can review.

entries.question: What is an MS relapse versus a pseudo-relapse?
entries.canonical_answer: A true MS relapse is a new or clearly worsening symptom that lasts more than a day and isn't explained by heat or infection. A pseudo-relapse is a temporary worsening of old symptoms triggered by things like heat, fever, or being run down, which improves once the trigger resolves. If you're unsure, contact your care team.

entries.question: When should I call about my MS symptoms?
entries.canonical_answer: It's reasonable to call your care team about new or worsening neurological symptoms that last more than a day, or anything that worries you. For sudden severe symptoms like loss of vision, severe weakness, or trouble speaking, treat it as urgent and seek emergency care.

entries.question: How long does it take to recover from a concussion?
entries.canonical_answer: Most people recover from a concussion over days to a few weeks, often starting with relative rest and then a gradual, guided return to activity. Recovery varies from person to person, so check in with your care team about your progress.

entries.question: When can I return to screens, sports, or work after a concussion?
entries.canonical_answer: Return to screens, sports, work, or school after a concussion is usually gradual and stepwise, increasing activity as long as symptoms don't flare. Because the right pace is individual, and returning to contact sports too soon carries real risk, follow the plan from your care team.

entries.question: What concussion symptoms are an emergency?
entries.canonical_answer: After a head injury, seek emergency care right away for a worsening or very severe headache, repeated vomiting, increasing confusion or trouble staying awake, slurred speech, weakness or numbness, seizures, or unequal pupils. When in doubt, call 911.

entries.question: I take seizure medicine and might become pregnant. What should I do?
entries.canonical_answer: If you take seizure medicine and are pregnant or thinking about it, talk with your neurologist before making any change, since planning ahead matters and some medicines carry different risks in pregnancy. Don't stop your medicine on your own, because uncontrolled seizures also carry risk.

entries.question: Do seizure medicines affect birth control?
entries.canonical_answer: Some seizure medicines can interact with hormonal birth control, making either one less effective, while others don't. Because it depends on your specific medicine, check with your prescriber or pharmacist about the best contraception for you.

entries.question: What is a seizure rescue medicine?
entries.canonical_answer: A seizure rescue medicine is a fast-acting treatment, often given into the nose or cheek, used to stop a prolonged seizure or a cluster of seizures as part of a personalized seizure action plan. If you think you might need one, ask your neurologist whether a rescue medicine and action plan are right for you.

entries.question: What daily safety tips help if I have seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: Common safety steps include taking showers instead of baths, being cautious around heights, open water, and heavy machinery, and not swimming alone. The right precautions depend on how well your seizures are controlled, so your care team can personalize them.

entries.question: When is dizziness serious?
entries.canonical_answer: Most dizziness isn't dangerous, but call 911 if it comes on suddenly with stroke-like signs such as slurred speech, face or limb weakness, double vision, a severe headache, or trouble walking. Otherwise, ongoing or bothersome dizziness is worth discussing with your care team.

entries.question: What is vertigo or BPPV?
entries.canonical_answer: Vertigo is a sensation that you or the room is spinning, and a common cause is BPPV, a usually harmless inner-ear problem triggered by certain head movements. Many causes are treatable, so an evaluation can help find the reason for yours.

entries.question: What is sciatica or a pinched nerve?
entries.canonical_answer: Sciatica, or a pinched nerve in the spine, happens when a nerve root is irritated or compressed, causing pain, numbness, or tingling that can travel down an arm or leg. Many cases improve over time, and your care team can guide treatment.

entries.question: When is back pain with leg symptoms an emergency?
entries.canonical_answer: Back pain becomes an emergency if you develop new loss of bladder or bowel control, numbness in the groin or saddle area, or rapidly worsening leg weakness. These can signal serious nerve compression, so call 911 or go to the emergency room right away.

entries.question: What does apixaban (Eliquis) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Apixaban, sold as Eliquis, is a blood thinner, or anticoagulant, that helps prevent blood clots and is often used to lower stroke risk in conditions like atrial fibrillation. Take it exactly as prescribed and tell your other providers and dentists you're on it, since it affects bleeding.

entries.question: What does warfarin (Coumadin) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Warfarin, sold as Coumadin, is a blood thinner used to prevent clots and strokes. It usually needs regular blood tests, called INR checks, and is affected by diet and other medicines, so consistency and follow-up matter. Take it exactly as prescribed.

entries.question: What precautions should I take on a blood thinner?
entries.canonical_answer: On a blood thinner it's important to watch for unusual bleeding, tell all your providers and dentists that you take it, and be careful to avoid falls and injuries. If you have a significant head injury, severe or non-stopping bleeding, or black or bloody stools, seek emergency care.

entries.question: What is medication-overuse headache?
entries.canonical_answer: Medication-overuse headache, sometimes called rebound headache, can develop when acute pain or headache medicines are used too often, leading to more frequent headaches. If you're reaching for pain relievers many days a week, it's worth talking with your care team about a better plan.

entries.question: How should I care for my feet if I have neuropathy?
entries.canonical_answer: With neuropathy, especially numbness in the feet, it helps to check your feet daily for cuts or sores, wear well-fitting protective shoes, and take steps to prevent falls. Report any wound that isn't healing to your care team.

entries.question: What is a stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: A stroke happens when blood flow to part of the brain is suddenly cut off or a blood vessel bursts, so brain cells in that area are injured. It's a medical emergency, and fast treatment matters. Symptoms come on suddenly, which is why BE-FAST and calling 911 are so important.

entries.question: What's the difference between an ischemic and a hemorrhagic stroke?
entries.canonical_answer: An ischemic stroke is caused by a clot blocking blood flow to the brain and is the most common type, while a hemorrhagic stroke is caused by bleeding from a burst vessel. They can look similar at first, so a brain scan is used to tell them apart, and treatment differs.

entries.question: What is a brain aneurysm?
entries.canonical_answer: A brain aneurysm is a weak, balloon-like bulge in a blood vessel in the brain. Many never cause problems, but if one leaks or bursts it is a life-threatening emergency, often felt as a sudden worst-ever headache, which needs 911 right away. Your team can explain monitoring or treatment if one is found.

entries.question: What is fainting and when is it serious?
entries.canonical_answer: Fainting (syncope) is a brief loss of consciousness from a temporary drop in blood flow to the brain, often from standing up fast, dehydration, or a strong emotional or pain response. Seek urgent care if fainting comes with chest pain, palpitations, trouble breathing, injury, or happens during exercise, or if it keeps happening.

entries.question: What is Bell's palsy?
entries.canonical_answer: Bell's palsy is sudden weakness or drooping of one side of the face from irritation of the facial nerve, and it usually improves over weeks to months. Because facial drooping can also be a sign of stroke, a sudden droop should be evaluated urgently to be safe, especially if it comes with other stroke symptoms.

entries.question: What is a tension headache?
entries.canonical_answer: A tension headache is the most common type, usually felt as a dull, tight, band-like pressure on both sides of the head, often linked to stress, poor sleep, or muscle tension. They're generally not dangerous and respond to lifestyle measures and simple treatments your team can review.

entries.question: What is a cluster headache?
entries.canonical_answer: Cluster headaches are severe, one-sided headaches, often around the eye, that come in groups or 'clusters' over days to weeks, frequently with a watery eye or stuffy nose on that side. They're uncommon but very painful and treatable, so a neurologist's input helps.

entries.question: What commonly triggers migraines?
entries.canonical_answer: Common migraine triggers include stress, missed meals, poor or changed sleep, dehydration, certain foods or alcohol, hormonal changes, and bright light or strong smells. Triggers vary by person, so a headache diary can help you spot yours.

entries.question: How are migraines treated?
entries.canonical_answer: Migraine treatment generally has two parts: medicines taken to stop an attack once it starts, and, for frequent migraines, preventive treatments taken regularly to reduce how often they happen. Lifestyle steps and avoiding triggers help too. Your neurologist can build a plan for you.

entries.question: What medicines help prevent migraines?
entries.canonical_answer: Several types of medicine can reduce how often migraines happen, including certain blood-pressure medicines, some antidepressants, certain anti-seizure medicines, and newer migraine-specific preventives, plus Botox for chronic migraine. Which is best depends on you, so it's a discussion for your prescriber.

entries.question: What is a migraine aura?
entries.canonical_answer: An aura is a set of warning symptoms that can come before or with a migraine, most often visual, like flashing lights, zigzag lines, or blind spots, and sometimes tingling or speech changes, usually lasting under an hour. Because aura-like symptoms can also signal a stroke, a first-ever or very different episode should be checked.

entries.question: What does sumatriptan (Imitrex) do?
entries.canonical_answer: Sumatriptan, sold as Imitrex, is a 'triptan' used to stop a migraine attack once it has started by narrowing blood vessels and calming nerve signals. It's taken at the start of an attack, not daily. It isn't right for everyone, so use it as prescribed.

entries.question: What does amitriptyline do for neurology?
entries.canonical_answer: Amitriptyline is an older medicine that, at low doses, is often used in neurology to help prevent migraines or ease certain nerve pain, and it can aid sleep. It's taken regularly, not just when symptoms hit. Your prescriber can explain what to expect.

entries.question: What does propranolol do for migraines?
entries.canonical_answer: Propranolol is a beta-blocker that, taken daily, can reduce how often migraines occur. It also lowers blood pressure and heart rate, so your team will check those. It's a preventive, not a treatment for an attack in progress.

entries.question: What does Botox do for migraines?
entries.canonical_answer: For chronic migraine (headaches on many days each month), Botox is given as a series of small injections around the head and neck every few months to reduce how often migraines happen. It's used for prevention, not to stop a single attack. A headache specialist can say if you're a candidate.

entries.question: What is epilepsy?
entries.canonical_answer: Epilepsy is a condition in which a person tends to have repeated seizures, which are bursts of abnormal electrical activity in the brain. It has many causes and is often well controlled with medication. A neurologist guides diagnosis and treatment.

entries.question: What are the different types of seizures?
entries.canonical_answer: Broadly, seizures are grouped into focal seizures, which start in one part of the brain and may or may not affect awareness, and generalized seizures, which involve both sides and can cause stiffening and shaking or staring spells. Knowing the type guides treatment, which is why descriptions of your events help your neurologist.

entries.question: What should I do after a first-ever seizure?
entries.canonical_answer: A first-ever seizure should be evaluated promptly, and if someone is actively having their first seizure you should call 911. After it, a medical work-up usually looks for a cause and decides whether treatment is needed. Write down what happened, since those details help the evaluation.

entries.question: What are the main symptoms of Parkinson's disease?
entries.canonical_answer: Common Parkinson's symptoms include a tremor at rest (often in a hand), slowness of movement, muscle stiffness, and balance or walking changes, and they usually start gradually on one side. Non-movement symptoms like sleep, mood, or smell changes can also occur. A neurologist makes the diagnosis.

entries.question: Does exercise help Parkinson's disease?
entries.canonical_answer: Yes, regular exercise is an important part of managing Parkinson's and can help with movement, balance, flexibility, and mood. Many people benefit from physical therapy or programs designed for Parkinson's. Your care team can suggest what's safe for you.

entries.question: What is deep brain stimulation?
entries.canonical_answer: Deep brain stimulation, or DBS, is a treatment for some people with Parkinson's or certain tremors, where a small implanted device sends electrical signals to specific brain areas to improve movement symptoms. It's considered in selected cases, and your neurologist can explain whether it might fit.

entries.question: What is restless legs syndrome?
entries.canonical_answer: Restless legs syndrome is an uncomfortable urge to move the legs, usually in the evening or at rest, that eases with movement and can disrupt sleep. It has several causes, including low iron in some people, and there are effective treatments. Your team can help find the cause.

entries.question: Why do my MS symptoms get worse in the heat?
entries.canonical_answer: Many people with MS notice symptoms temporarily worsen with heat, fever, or overheating, a reversible effect sometimes called Uhthoff's phenomenon. Cooling down usually brings symptoms back to baseline. Because a true relapse is different, let your team know if symptoms don't recover after you cool off.

entries.question: How is multiple sclerosis treated?
entries.canonical_answer: MS care usually includes disease-modifying treatments that aim to reduce relapses and slow progression, treatments to manage specific symptoms, and rehabilitation. The right plan is individualized, so your neurologist will tailor it to your type of MS and goals.

entries.question: How can I manage MS fatigue?
entries.canonical_answer: Fatigue is one of the most common MS symptoms. Strategies that help many people include pacing activities, prioritizing rest, staying cool, regular gentle exercise, and good sleep, and sometimes treating other contributors like mood or sleep problems. Your team can help build a plan.

entries.question: What causes neuropathy?
entries.canonical_answer: Neuropathy has many possible causes, including diabetes, vitamin deficiencies, certain medicines or toxins, infections, and other medical conditions, and sometimes no cause is found. Identifying the cause matters because some are treatable, so an evaluation is worthwhile.

entries.question: Are there medications for memory loss or dementia?
entries.canonical_answer: There are medicines that can help with symptoms of certain types of dementia for some people, and newer treatments exist for early Alzheimer's in selected cases, though none are a cure. Treating contributing factors and supportive care also matter. A specialist can discuss what fits.
